unit.subject: English
unit.unitTitle: My Year of Words: Grade 3 Capstone
unit.unitOverview: Unit 10 is the last unit of Year 3, and it gathers up the whole year. You revise thirty words from Units 1 to 8, from family and library to sailor, kindness, temperature and million. Six grammar workshops bring back the past simple, the present forms, questions, comparatives, the joining words and reported speech. Then you plan, draft, revise and publish a six-page booklet called My Year of Words, and you present it at the Year 3 Showcase, just as Amal does in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Read "Amal's Year of Words", the Showcase brief, the poem "Nine Doors" and the two listening texts, then answer the twelve comprehension questions.
Revise the thirty review words and build your own word map, unit by unit.
Work through the six cumulative grammar workshops, one at a time and never all at once.
Plan, draft, revise and publish the six-page My Year of Words booklet.
Rehearse across the six live sessions and deliver your two-minute showcase presentation.
Finish with the capstone quiz, the portfolio assignment and your Year 3 reflection.
unit.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

visual.alt: An illustrated child standing beside a display table, presenting a finished English booklet

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The people who live with you and love you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My family has my mum, my dad, my sister, and me.
practiceSentences: Amal put a family tree at the front of her folder.
Every family in our class received a showcase invitation card.
Which family member will you invite to the showcase evening?
Grandma Hana said, "Our family loves your careful writing."
I read my Year 3 report to my family last night.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - a - m - i - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me who is in my family, then help me write one sentence about the family tree page in my showcase booklet.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A mother or a father who looks after you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My parents take me to school every morning.
practiceSentences: Each parent signs the showcase list beside the classroom door.
One parent helped us carry the display boards into the garden.
Has your parent booked a seat for showcase evening?
Teacher Yasmin said, "Every parent is welcome on Friday."
I showed my parent the poem I wrote in March.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - a - r - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult sitting with me, ask me what a parent does each day, then check that I say parent for one person and parents for two.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person you like and spend time with.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My friend Nora and I play football after school.
practiceSentences: Nora is the friend who checked my spelling all year.
A good friend gives kind advice before the final draft.
Which friend will help you rehearse your showcase talk?
Sami said, "My friend Leo drew the border for me."
I thanked my friend for listening to my nervous practice.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - r - i - e - n - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult nearby, ask me to name one friend who helped my project this year and to say exactly what that friend did.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who goes to school to learn new things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Every student in my class can read now.
practiceSentences: Every student chooses six pages for the Year 3 showcase.
One new student joined our class in February this year.
Which student will speak first on showcase evening?
Teacher Yasmin said, "Each student has grown this year."
I am the student who looks after our class library.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - u - d - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me what a student does that a visitor does not, then help me use student in one showcase sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who helps us learn new things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My teacher helps me with my mathematics homework.
practiceSentences: Our teacher gave every child one large green folder.
A teacher reads each draft and writes one helpful target.
Did the teacher explain the four parts of the project?
Nora said, "Our teacher listens to every single idea."
I asked my teacher to check my past tense verbs.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - a - c - h - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me to describe one thing Teacher Yasmin did this year, and help me spell teacher without looking.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who writes books or stories.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The author wrote a funny book about a talking camel.
practiceSentences: Amal signed her booklet the way a real author would.
An author plans the order of the ideas before writing.
Which author did you enjoy reading most this year?
Maya said, "I want to be an author one day."
I wrote my name as the author on the title page.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - u - t - h - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult with me, ask me why my name goes under the word author on my title page, then help me name one author I read this year.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A place where you can find and borrow books.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I go to the library to read and borrow books.
practiceSentences: The school library will hold our showcase if rain comes.
Quiet voices keep the library calm while other children read.
May we practise our showcase talks in the library?
Leo said, "I found my best fact in the library."
I borrowed three books from the library in April.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - i - b - r - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me how the library is different from a bookshop, and listen to me spell library slowly.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To finish school or a class.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My sister will graduate from primary school next year.
practiceSentences: Our class will graduate from Year 3 at the end of term.
Students graduate only after finishing every part of the project.
When will your older brother graduate from college?
Adam said, "I will graduate before you reach Year 6."
I clapped when I watched my cousin graduate last year.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - r - a - d - u - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult sitting with me, ask me what graduate means and help me say one sentence about graduating from Year 3.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The first month of the year.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The school year begins in January.
practiceSentences: In January Amal could hardly write one clear paragraph.
The showcase planning began quietly back in January.
What was the hardest lesson for you in January?
Grandma Hana said, "You have grown so much since January."
I keep my January goal card inside my green folder.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: J - a - n - u - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me what I could not do in January that I can do now, then check my capital letter on January.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The second month of the year, and the shortest with twenty-eight days.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: February is the shortest month.
practiceSentences: Our class learned the twelve months during February.
February gave us twenty-eight days of reading practice.
Did you finish your calendar chart in February?
Maya said, "February felt short but very busy."
I coloured the February square green on my chart.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: F - e - b - r - u - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me how many days February usually has, then help me spell the tricky letter r in the middle.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The third month of the year, when heavy rains often arrive.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The rains often come in March.
practiceSentences: Heavy rain kept our class indoors for most of March.
March sits between February and April on our wall calendar.
Which story did you enjoy most during March?
Theo said, "We planted the garden beds in March."
I wrote my first full report during rainy March.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: M - a - r - c - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me which months come before and after March, and help me use March in one weather sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The fourth month of the year, when flowers bloom in many places.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We plant our garden in April.
practiceSentences: The class garden turned bright green during April.
Gentle April showers helped our young seedlings grow tall.
Was the book fair held in April this year?
Nora said, "April was the month I read longer books."
I measured the tallest sunflower on the last April morning.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: A - p - r - i - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me what our class garden looked like in April, then check that I write April with one capital letter.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who helps sick people feel better.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The doctor works at the hospital.
practiceSentences: Doctor Sarah answered every question our class asked in Unit 4.
A doctor listens carefully before giving any advice at all.
Will the doctor visit our showcase on Friday evening?
Sami said, "The doctor showed us how to wash our hands."
I drew a doctor on my community helpers page.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - o - c - t - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult with me, ask me what Doctor Sarah does at the clinic, then help me use doctor and hospital in the same sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who helps keep people safe and makes sure rules are followed.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The officer helped us cross the road.
practiceSentences: An officer stopped the traffic while our class crossed safely.
The officer keeps the road outside our school gate safe.
Where does the traffic officer stand each school morning?
Theo said, "The officer waved to us from the corner."
I wrote three sentences about the officer in my report.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: o - f - f - i - c - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult nearby, ask me one job an officer does, then remind me that officer needs an before it because it starts with a vowel sound.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who works on a boat or ship at sea.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sailor sails across the sea.
practiceSentences: A sailor reads the wind and the stars at night.
The sailor in our poem carried letters across the sea.
Has any sailor ever visited your school before?
Idris said, "I want to be a sailor one day."
I drew a sailor beside my painted boat picture.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - a - i - l - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me where a sailor works and what a sailor needs, then help me spell sailor sound by sound.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A place where doctors and nurses care for people who are sick or hurt.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My sister is resting at the hospital.
practiceSentences: Our class report described the county hospital and its children's ward.
The hospital stays open at every hour of the night.
Which bus stops outside the hospital gate?
Mina said, "The hospital garden has bright yellow flowers."
I took my hospital drawing out for the showcase table.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - o - s - p - i - t - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me who works in a hospital, then help me count the syllables in hospital before I spell it.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To talk about something with other people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will discuss our ideas in a group.
practiceSentences: Groups discuss their plans before anybody starts building.
We will discuss the showcase order after morning break.
Shall we discuss which pages to display first?
Teacher Yasmin said, "Discuss it with your group first."
I discuss my drafts with Nora every Thursday afternoon.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - s - c - u - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult with me, ask me to discuss my two best pages with you and to give one reason for each choice.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To use your ears to hear carefully.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Please listen to the teacher when she talks.
practiceSentences: We listen to every plan before our group chooses one.
Visitors listen quietly while each child presents a booklet.
Do you listen for the bell at the end of break?
Teacher Yasmin said, "Listen to everybody's plan today."
I listen to my recording twice before I change anything.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - i - s - t - e - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, read my showcase talk aloud and ask me to listen for one sentence I should say more slowly.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To say kindly that you will give or do something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I will offer help if my friend falls down.
practiceSentences: Nora will offer to carry the heavy display board outside.
Older pupils offer help to nervous Year 3 speakers.
Will you offer your seat to a visiting parent?
Amal said, "I offer to help a new student next year."
I offer my spare pencil whenever somebody forgets one.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: o - f - f - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me to offer help to somebody at the showcase using a polite full sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make something by putting parts together.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will build a wall with smooth stones.
practiceSentences: Sami asked whether the class could build one folder stand.
The groups build their display tables from strong cardboard boxes.
Can we build the stand before Friday morning?
Maya said, "Let us build the tallest tower for the poems."
I helped build the reading wall behind our school garden.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - u - i - l - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult with me, ask me the past form of build, then help me say one sentence about what our class built this year.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Nice and helpful to other people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The kind girl shared her lunch with a new pupil.
practiceSentences: Nora wrote one kind comment on every partner's draft.
Kind words help a nervous speaker begin again calmly.
What kind thing did somebody do for you today?
Grandma Hana said, "Kind hands help before anybody asks."
I try to be kind when my little sister interrupts.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: k - i - n - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me for one kind sentence I could say to a partner about their booklet.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Happy to talk to people and play with them.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Our new neighbour is very friendly.
practiceSentences: Leo greeted every showcase visitor with a friendly smile.
A friendly voice makes the first sentence much easier.
Are the older pupils friendly at the showcase evening?
Teacher Yasmin said, "Be friendly to anybody who looks lost."
I felt friendly and calm once the clapping started.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - r - i - e - n - d - l - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me the difference between kind and friendly, then help me use friendly about a visitor.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Always telling the truth, even when it is hard.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Amal is honest, so she told the teacher what really happened.
practiceSentences: Amal wrote one honest sentence on her kindness jar page.
An honest reflection names the hard parts as well.
Was your reflection honest about the pages you found difficult?
Nora said, "Honest feedback helped my writing most this year."
I gave an honest answer about my weakest showcase page.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - o - n - e - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me to say one honest sentence about a page I found hard, and remind me that honest begins with a quiet h.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The act of being kind and helpful to somebody.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She showed kindness by helping the boy who fell.
practiceSentences: Amal kept her kindness jar page for the final showcase.
Small acts of kindness filled our jar by the last week.
Which act of kindness will you write about tonight?
Teacher Yasmin thanked the class for their kindness to Amal.
I counted every kindness note before I sealed the jar.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: k - i - n - d - n - e - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult with me, ask me how kind becomes kindness, then help me name one act of kindness from this term.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The usual weather in a place over a long time.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The desert has a hot, dry climate.
practiceSentences: Our poster compared the coast climate with the mountain climate.
A dry climate keeps the village paths dusty for months.
Does the climate change as you climb higher up?
Sami said, "Our warm climate suits an outdoor showcase."
I explained our climate chart to every showcase visitor.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - l - i - m - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me how climate is different from today's weather, then help me describe our own climate.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: What the sky and air are like today.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Today the weather is sunny and warm.
practiceSentences: We checked the weather before choosing the school garden.
Wet weather would move the whole showcase into the library.
Will the weather stay dry until Friday evening?
Maya said, "I recorded the weather for seven days."
I stuck my weather chart beside my climate poster.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - e - a - t - h - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me to describe today's weather in two sentences and to use one describing word in each.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How hot or cold something is.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The temperature is 30 degrees today.
practiceSentences: Amal wrote the temperature in careful numbers for one week.
The temperature rises quickly in our classroom after noon.
Which day had the highest temperature on your chart?
Nora said, "The temperature dropped when the rain arrived."
I measured the temperature outside the library door.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - m - p - e - r - a - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult with me, ask me what the temperature was this morning, then help me clap the four beats in temperature.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The light and warmth that come from the sun.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sunshine helped the flowers grow.
practiceSentences: Bright sunshine filled the school garden on showcase morning.
Warm sunshine dried the painted signs within one hour.
Do the sunflowers need sunshine every single day?
Theo said, "Sunshine after rain always makes a rainbow."
I sat in the sunshine and rehearsed my two minutes.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - n - s - h - i - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, ask me which two smaller words hide inside sunshine, then help me use sunshine in one garden sentence.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A word or symbol that shows how many things there are.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My favourite number is seven.
practiceSentences: Each folder carries one number on its top corner.
The number of showcase visitors grew larger every year.
Which number did you write on your display table?
Sami said, "Double the number and add one more chair."
I checked every number on my measuring page twice.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - u - m - b - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me to read the numbers on my measuring page aloud and to say which one was hardest to write.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very big number written 1,000,000, or one thousand thousands.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are more than a million stars in the sky.
practiceSentences: Amal filled one whole page with numbers up to a million.
A million seconds is more than eleven whole days.
How many zeros does the number million need?
Nora said, "I spelt million wrongly on my first try."
I still remember how to spell million correctly today.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - i - l - l - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult with me, ask me how many zeros a million has, then help me write million with the double letter in the middle.

rubrics.level4: Review words used precisely and naturally

rubrics.level2: Responds with one or two words

rubrics.level4: Ideas are clear, relevant and developed
Clear order, paragraphs and linking openers

readings.title: Amal's Year of Words
readings.setting: The Year 3 classroom and Amal's home
readings.passageScript:
It was the last Monday of Year 3. Teacher Yasmin put one large green folder on every desk.
"Inside this folder is your whole year," she said. "Choose the work that shows how far you have travelled."
Amal opened her own folder slowly. On the very first page she found her family tree from Unit 1. She had drawn her parents, her older brother Adam and her little sister Mina. Grandma Hana sat in the corner with her reading glasses.
Behind the family tree lay her calendar chart. She had coloured January blue, February green and March grey for the heavy rains. Under the chart she found her report about the county hospital. Doctor Sarah had answered every question the class asked.
Then Amal turned to another page. There was her poster about climate and weather. She had written the temperature in careful numbers for a whole week. Next came her page of big numbers, all the way up to a million.
"I was so proud of this one," Amal whispered to Nora.
Nora leaned across the wooden desk. "You spelt million with two letters l," she said. "I remember, because I spelt it wrongly first."
Amal laughed and made a small tick beside the page.
At the back she found her kindness jar page from Unit 6. On it she had written one honest sentence. "Being friendly is easy on good days and hard on tired days." Reading it again, Amal decided to keep that page for her showcase.
Then Sami raised his hand at the front. "Teacher Yasmin, may we build a stand for our folders?"
"You may build one," said Teacher Yasmin. "Discuss it with your group first, and listen to everybody's plan."
That evening Amal showed the folder to Grandma Hana.
"In January you could hardly write one paragraph," Grandma Hana said. "Now you write a whole page."
Amal smiled and picked up her pencil. On the last sheet she wrote her goal for Grade 4. "Next year I will read a long chapter book by myself. I will also offer to help a new student on the first day."
readings.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

readings.title: The Year 3 Showcase: Project Brief
readings.passageScript:
The Year 3 Showcase is the last project of your English year. It has four parts, and you complete them in order.

Part 1: Choose your pages. Pick six pages from at least four different units. Look for work that shows reading, writing, speaking and new words.

Part 2: Build your booklet. Write two full paragraphs on every page. Add one picture and one clear label to each page. Put your name and your class on the title page. Then write the word Author underneath your name.

Part 3: Present your work. Speak for about two minutes beside your display table. Point at your pictures and answer two questions from your listeners.

Part 4: Reflect. Write four honest sentences about what you can do now. Then write one goal for Grade 4.

Hand the finished booklet to your teacher on the last Friday of the term. Read every page aloud before you hand it in. Your ears will find the missing full stops for you. Ask a partner for one kind idea and one careful question. Change what you can, and keep the pages that are already strong.
readings.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

readings.title: A Poem: "Nine Doors"
readings.passageScript:
Nine doors stood open along our Year 3 hall,
and I walked through every single one of them.

Door One kept my family safely up ahead,
my parents, my duty and the words my grandma said.

Door Two was the library, cool and wide,
where an author and a lesson waited side by side.

Door Three showed a calendar upon the classroom wall:
January, February, March and April, standing tall.

Door Four opened wide upon the busy town,
the hospital, the doctor, the officer, the sailor going down.

Door Five said build, discuss, offer, listen,
until the stones of our new garden wall began to glisten.

Door Six was full of kindness, honest, warm and kind,
with one friendly word for anybody left behind.

Door Seven blew the weather right across the coast,
the climate, the temperature, the sunshine I love most.

Door Eight counted every number, small and tall,
from one, to one hundred, to a million counting all.

Door Nine stayed quiet, for thinking and for dreams,
for ideas and for choices and for careful little schemes.

Nine doors, one long year, one growing Year 3 voice.
Tomorrow I walk onward, and Grade 4 is my choice.
readings.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

readings.title: Planning the Showcase
readings.passageScript:
Teacher Yasmin: We have one week left, so let us plan the showcase carefully.
Amal: Where will we put the display tables?
Teacher Yasmin: In the school garden, if the weather stays dry.
Maya: I measured the long table this morning. It is two metres and forty centimetres.
Sami: Then we can fit six booklets on it, with one small gap between each.
Nora: How many chairs will the visitors need?
Teacher Yasmin: Twenty-eight chairs, because every parent has answered the invitation.
Amal: Shall I check the temperature chart for Friday?
Teacher Yasmin: Yes, please. Sami, will you build the folder stand?
Sami: I will build it with Leo, and we will use the strong boxes.
Maya: What happens if the heavy rain comes back?
Teacher Yasmin: Then we move everything into the library, and the showcase still goes ahead.
Nora: I will offer to carry the heavy display boards.
Teacher Yasmin: Thank you, Nora. Now discuss the order of speakers in your groups.
readings.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

readings.title: Showcase Day
readings.setting: The school garden on showcase evening
readings.passageScript:
Teacher Yasmin: Welcome, everybody. Amal will present first.
Amal: Good evening. My name is Amal, and my booklet is called My Year of Words.
Grandma Hana: Which page are you most proud of, Amal?
Amal: Page five, because it holds my kindness jar and one honest sentence.
Doctor Sarah: Which page was the hardest for you to finish?
Amal: The climate poster was hardest, because I recorded the temperature for seven days.
Doctor Sarah: That sounds like careful science as well as careful English.
Adam: How did you make your family page better?
Amal: I read it aloud to you, and you said the middle was too short. Then I added two sentences about Mina.
Leo: I like your million page. The numbers march all the way across it.
Amal: Thank you. I checked every number twice before I glued the page down.
Teacher Yasmin: What is your goal for Grade 4, Amal?
Amal: I will read a long chapter book by myself. I will also offer to help a new student.
Teacher Yasmin: Thank you, Amal. Nora, you may present now.
readings.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: What did Teacher Yasmin put on every desk, and what did she tell the class to choose?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She put one large green folder on every desk, and she told them to choose the work that shows how far they have travelled.
comprehension.explanation: The opening two sentences name the green folder and quote the instruction about choosing work.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: Nora looks at the word million on Amal's page. What does she say about it, and why does she remember?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She says Amal spelt million with two letters l, and she remembers because she spelt it wrongly first.
comprehension.explanation: Nora's two spoken lines give both the spelling detail and her reason for remembering it.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: Grandma Hana compares January with now. What does her comparison tell you about Amal's writing?
comprehension.correctAnswer: In January Amal could hardly write one paragraph, and now she writes a whole page, so her writing has grown a lot.
comprehension.explanation: The child must join Grandma Hana's two sentences and draw the conclusion the text does not state.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: Amal writes her Grade 4 goal on the last sheet. What two promises does she make?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She will read a long chapter book by herself, and she will offer to help a new student on the first day.
comprehension.explanation: The final sentence of the story quotes both halves of the goal, joined by and.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: The Showcase brief lists four parts. What must you do in Part 3?
comprehension.correctAnswer: You speak for about two minutes beside your display table, point at your pictures and answer two questions.
comprehension.explanation: Part 3 of the brief gives the time, the place and the two listener questions.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: What three things must go on the title page of your booklet?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Your name, your class and the word Author written underneath.
comprehension.explanation: Part 2 of the brief names all three, so the child scans that paragraph rather than reading it all.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: In the poem "Nine Doors", which four months does Door Three name?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Door Three names January, February, March and April.
comprehension.explanation: The Door Three verse lists the four months in calendar order on its second line.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: The poem gives each unit its own door. What does the last verse say the nine doors gave the speaker?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The last verse says the nine doors gave the speaker one growing Year 3 voice, ready to walk onward into Grade 4.
comprehension.explanation: The closing couplet turns the doors into the speaker's growing voice and a choice about next year.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: How long is the table Maya measured, and how many booklets will fit on it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The table is two metres and forty centimetres long, and six booklets will fit on it.
comprehension.explanation: Maya gives the measurement and Sami answers with the number of booklets in the next line.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: Why does Teacher Yasmin ask for twenty-eight chairs?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because every parent has answered the invitation, so she knows exactly how many visitors are coming.
comprehension.explanation: Teacher Yasmin gives the reason with because, and the child must see that the number comes from the replies.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: Which page is Amal most proud of, and what two things does that page hold?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Page five, because it holds her kindness jar and one honest sentence.
comprehension.explanation: Amal answers Grandma Hana by naming page five and the two things on it.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

comprehension.question: Adam asks how Amal improved her family page. What feedback did she act on?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Adam told her the middle was too short, so she added two sentences about Mina.
comprehension.explanation: Amal names the feedback and the exact change she made because of it.
comprehension.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

grammar.title: Past Simple Review: Saying What We Did
grammar.explanation: The past simple tells what already happened and finished. You met it first in Unit 2, and used it again in Units 3, 5 and 6. Regular verbs add -ed, while a small group of very common verbs change shape completely.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Regular verbs add -ed: discuss becomes discussed, listen becomes listened, offer becomes offered.
Irregular verbs change completely: build becomes built, go becomes went, see becomes saw, eat becomes ate.
Negatives use did not plus the plain verb: "We did not build the stand on Monday."
Time words help your reader: last March, yesterday, in February, at the end of term.
grammar.commonMistake:
After did, learners often keep the -ed ending and write "We did not built it." This is not right yet.
The helper did already carries the past, so the verb stays plain: "We did not build it."
grammar.memoryTip: Put the word yesterday in front of your sentence. If yesterday fits, the verb must be in its past form.
grammar.practice: Write each verb in the past simple. | Last February our class ______ (listen) to nine stories. | Sami ______ (build) the folder stand with Leo. | Teacher Yasmin ______ (offer) us one extra week. | We ______ (see) the whole year inside one green folder.
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

grammar.title: Present Simple and Present Continuous Review
grammar.explanation: The present simple tells what is true every day, and the present continuous tells what is happening right now. Unit 1 taught the continuous form, and Units 3 and 4 practised the simple form until it felt easy.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Present simple: use the plain verb with I, you, we and they, as in "They listen carefully."
Add -s or -es with he, she and it, as in "She listens carefully." and "He goes to the library."
Present continuous: use am, is or are plus the verb with -ing, as in "I am building my display table."
Use the continuous only for this moment, as in "Nora is presenting her booklet right now."
grammar.commonMistake:
Learners sometimes drop the helper and write "She presenting her page now." The sentence then has no verb tense.
The continuous always needs am, is or are in front: "She is presenting her page now."
grammar.memoryTip: Every day takes the plain form; right now takes -ing with a helper standing in front of it.
grammar.practice: Choose the present simple or the present continuous. | Our teacher ______ (check) every draft on Fridays. | Look, Maya ______ (measure) the long table now. | Every student ______ (choose) six pages for the showcase. | Listen, the visitors ______ (clap) for Nora.
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

grammar.title: Questions and Short Answers Review
grammar.explanation: Units 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 and 7 all asked you to build questions. Every English question puts a helper word in front of the subject, and short answers reuse that same helper.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Wh- questions ask for information with Who, What, Where, When, Why and How.
The order is Wh- word, helper, subject, plain verb: "Where did you find that fact?"
Yes/No questions begin with the helper: "Did the officer visit our class?" and "Have you finished your booklet?"
Short answers reuse the helper: "Yes, I have." and "No, we did not."
grammar.commonMistake:
Learners often keep statement order and write "Where you did find that fact?" The words are right but the order is not.
The helper jumps in front of the subject: "Where did you find that fact?"
grammar.memoryTip: Find the helper first. In every English question the helper hops over the subject and lands in front of it.
grammar.practice: Turn each statement into the question asked for. | Amal recorded the temperature for seven days. (Ask with How long.) | The class will graduate at the end of term. (Ask with When.) | Nora has finished her honest reflection. (Ask a Yes or No question.) | Sami built the folder stand with Leo. (Ask with Who.)
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

grammar.title: Adjectives, Comparatives and Noun Phrases Review
grammar.explanation: Unit 4 taught describing words and their opposites, Unit 6 taught how to compare two people, and Unit 7 taught how to build longer describing phrases. This workshop puts all three together.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
One adjective describes one noun: "the busy market" and "the honest answer".
Two adjectives take a comma between them: "the large, quiet library".
Short adjectives compare with -er: kind becomes kinder, warm becomes warmer, busy becomes busier.
Longer adjectives compare with more: helpful becomes more helpful, popular becomes more popular.
grammar.commonMistake:
Learners sometimes use both forms at once and write "The garden is more warmer today." Only one comparing signal is needed.
Choose one form and stop there: "The garden is warmer today."
grammar.memoryTip: Clap the beats in the adjective. One or two beats usually take -er, and three or more take the word more.
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with the correct describing form. | March was ______ (wet) than April this year. | Our library is the ______ (quiet) room in the whole school. | Nora is ______ (helpful) than she was back in January. | Write one noun phrase with two adjectives about your display table.
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

grammar.title: Joining Ideas with and, but, so and because
grammar.explanation: Unit 1 taught and, Units 4 and 5 taught because and so, and Unit 9 added but. Choosing the right joining word shows the reader how your two ideas are linked.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
and adds another idea: "I chose my poem and my climate poster."
but shows a difference: "I liked the poem, but the poster was harder."
so shows the result: "The rain stopped, so we moved outside."
because gives the reason: "We measured the table because we needed the right size."
grammar.commonMistake:
Learners sometimes join every idea with and, which builds one very long breathless sentence.
Break the sentence up and pick the word that shows the real link: but, so or because.
grammar.memoryTip: because looks backwards to the reason, and so looks forwards to the result. Point with your finger as you choose.
grammar.practice: Join each pair with and, but, so or because. | The weather stayed dry. We held the showcase in the garden. | Amal felt nervous. She spoke clearly. | I checked every number twice. I did not want mistakes. | Sami built the stand. Leo painted the labels.
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

grammar.title: Imperatives, Reported Speech and Final Proofreading
grammar.explanation: Units 1, 3 and 7 taught instructions, Units 6 and 9 taught how to report what somebody said, and every unit asked you to proofread. This last workshop is your editing toolkit for the showcase booklet.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Imperatives begin with the plain verb and leave out the word you: "Read every page aloud."
For a do-not command, begin with Do not: "Do not glue the labels before the paint dries."
Reported speech begins with said, told or asked, and the verb steps back in time: "I am ready" becomes "She said she was ready."
Proofreading marks to check: capital letter, full stop, question mark, speech marks.
grammar.commonMistake:
Learners often forget to change the pronoun and write "Sami said I will build it." The reader then thinks Sami meant somebody else.
Match the pronoun to the new speaker: "Sami said he would build it."
grammar.memoryTip: Read your work aloud slowly. Your ears will hear the missing full stop long before your eyes see it.
grammar.practice: Rewrite each line as instructed. | "I am proud of my booklet," said Amal. (Report it.) | "We will listen to every plan," said the class. (Report it.) | Tell a partner to check the title page. (Write it as an imperative.) | Tell a partner not to rush the last paragraph. (Write it as a negative imperative.)
grammar.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Choosing My Six Pages
speaking.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose three pages from your folder and explain each choice out loud.
"I chose my ______ page because it shows ______."
"This page was hard in ______, but it is easier now."
"The word I am most proud of using here is ______."
Record yourself. Check: did you name the page, give a reason with because, and name one review word?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult sitting beside me, listen to my three page choices and ask me for a stronger reason for the weakest one.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Reading My Best Page Aloud
speaking.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Read your strongest page aloud twice, then read it to a partner.
Pause at every full stop and lift your voice at every question mark.
Make the speech marks sound different from the rest, as if a person is really talking.
Record the second reading. Check: did you pause at the full stops and finish every word clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, listen to me read my best page aloud and tell me one place where I should pause a little longer.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Review Words in Real Sentences
speaking.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Pick eight review words from the thirty, then say one true sentence for each.
Use at least one word from Units 1 to 3, one from Units 4 to 6, and one from Units 7 and 8.
"Our ______ climate suits an outdoor showcase."
"I will offer to carry the display boards."
Check: were all eight sentences true, complete and different from each other?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult with me, pick eight review words at random and ask me to use each one in a true sentence about my year.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — My Two-Minute Showcase Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Plan a talk of about two minutes about your finished booklet.
Open: "Good evening. My name is ______, and my booklet is called ______."
Middle: describe three pages and say why each one matters.
Close: "My goal for Grade 4 is ______. Thank you for listening."
Check: did you look up at least three times and speak slowly enough to be heard?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside me, time my two-minute talk and tell me whether my opening and closing lines were clear.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Answering Listeners' Questions
speaking.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Ask a partner to prepare three questions about your booklet, then answer them without notes.
"That is a good question. The reason is ______."
"I am not sure, but I think ______."
"Would you like me to show you that page?"
Check: did you answer in full sentences and stay polite when you did not know?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask me three surprise questions about my booklet and check that I answer in full sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — Showcase Day Presentation
speaking.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Stand beside your display table and present your booklet to real visitors.
Greet each visitor, present for two minutes, then take two questions.
Thank every listener before the next child begins.
Check: did you greet, present, answer and thank, all without rushing?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult nearby, play the part of a showcase visitor, ask me two questions and tell me how confident I sounded.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Page Captions for My Booklet
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write one caption of two sentences for each of your six chosen pages. Say what the page shows and what it proves you can do now.
Start like this: "This page shows ______. It proves that I can ______."
writing.modelText: This page shows my temperature chart for one whole week. It proves that I can record numbers neatly and explain what they mean.
writing.expectedLength: Six captions of two sentences each
writing.successCriteria: I wrote six captions; every caption has two full sentences; each one names the page and what it proves; I used one review word in at least four of them.
writing.support: Say the caption aloud first, then write it exactly as you said it.
writing.extension: Add a third sentence to two captions explaining what you would change if you did the page again.

writing.title: Writing 2 — My Showcase Plan
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Plan your booklet before you draft it. List your six pages in order, and write one sentence under each saying what will go on it.
Use the four parts of the Showcase brief as your headings.
writing.modelText: Page one: my family tree. I will name my parents, Adam and Mina, and add one sentence about Grandma Hana.
writing.expectedLength: Six numbered pages with one planning sentence each
writing.successCriteria: I listed six pages in a sensible order; each page has one planning sentence; my plan follows the four parts of the brief.
writing.support: Lay your six real pages on the desk and move them around until the order feels right.
writing.extension: Add a note beside each page saying which unit it came from.

writing.title: Writing 3 — First Draft of My Year Report
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write the first draft of your year report in three paragraphs. Paragraph one: how you started Year 3. Paragraph two: what you learned. Paragraph three: what you can do now.
Use and, but, so and because to join your ideas.
writing.modelText: In January I could hardly write one paragraph, but I kept practising every week. By March I could join two ideas with because, so my writing sounded clearer.
writing.expectedLength: Three paragraphs of four to six sentences
writing.successCriteria: I wrote three paragraphs; each one has its own job; I used at least three different joining words; every sentence has a capital letter and an end mark.
writing.support: Write one paragraph a day rather than all three at once.
writing.extension: Open your second and third paragraphs with a time phrase, such as Later that term.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Revising After Peer Feedback
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Swap drafts with a partner. Write one kind idea and one careful question on their draft. Then revise your own draft using the feedback you were given.
Mark every change you make in a different colour.
writing.modelText: My partner asked which month I meant, so I changed "later" to "in April" and added the temperature I had recorded.
writing.expectedLength: One revised draft plus three marked changes
writing.successCriteria: I gave one kind idea and one careful question; I made at least three changes; I can explain why each change makes the writing better.
writing.support: Read your partner's draft aloud before you write anything on it.
writing.extension: Write two sentences explaining which change improved your report most.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Presentation Notes
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Turn your report into six short note cards, one for each page. Write no more than twelve words on a card, because notes are prompts and not a script.
Number the cards so they cannot get out of order.
writing.modelText: Card 3: climate poster, seven days of temperature, hardest page, kept going.
writing.expectedLength: Six numbered cards, twelve words or fewer on each
writing.successCriteria: I made six numbered cards; no card has more than twelve words; the cards follow the order of my booklet; I can speak from them without reading a script.
writing.support: Say the full sentence out loud, then keep only the words you would forget.
writing.extension: Add one arrow on each card to remind you where to point.

writing.title: Writing 6 — My Year 3 Reflection and Grade 4 Goal
writing.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write four honest sentences about your Year 3 English. Name one thing you found hard, one thing you improved, one page you are proud of, and one goal for Grade 4.
Be honest about the hard parts, because an honest reflection is the most useful one.
writing.modelText: Reading long texts was hard for me in January. My spelling improved most, especially the double letters in million.
writing.expectedLength: Four honest sentences, one per prompt
writing.successCriteria: I answered all four prompts; my reflection names something hard as well as something good; my Grade 4 goal is one clear action I can start in January.
writing.support: Talk each sentence through with an adult before you write it.
writing.extension: Add one sentence saying how you will know when your Grade 4 goal is reached.

activities.title: Activities 1 — Curate Six Pages from Nine Units
activities.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Spread all your Year 3 work on the desk and sort it into three piles: keep, maybe, and not this time.
Choose six pages from at least four different units.
Write the unit number on the back of every page you keep.
Check that your six pages include reading, writing, speaking and new words.
activities.answerSummary: Six pages selected from four or more different units, each labelled with its unit number, and all four skills represented across the set.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Build the Thirty-Word Review Map
activities.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Draw eight boxes and label them with the unit numbers 1 to 8.
Sort the thirty review words into the box of the unit that taught them.
Write one true sentence beside four of the boxes.
Circle the five words you still find hardest to spell.
activities.answerSummary: Family, parent, friend and student go in Unit 1; teacher, author, library and graduate in Unit 2; January, February, March and April in Unit 3; doctor, officer, sailor and hospital in Unit 4; discuss, listen, offer and build in Unit 5; kind, friendly, honest and kindness in Unit 6; climate, weather, temperature and sunshine in Unit 7; number and million in Unit 8.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Grammar Repair Shop
activities.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Repair each sentence, then say which grammar workshop it came from.
Last March we builded the folder stand.
She presenting her booklet right now.
Where you did find that fact?
The garden is more warmer than the hall.
activities.answerSummary: Last March we built the folder stand. She is presenting her booklet right now. Where did you find that fact? The garden is warmer than the hall.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Peer Feedback Studio
activities.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Work in threes. Read one booklet at a time, all the way through, without speaking.
Each reader writes one kind idea on a green sticky note.
Each reader writes one careful question on a yellow sticky note.
The writer chooses two notes to act on and explains the choice to the group.
activities.answerSummary: Every booklet leaves the studio with at least four notes, two green and two yellow, and the writer can name the two they will act on and why.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Timed Rehearsal with a Stopwatch
activities.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Rehearse your talk three times while a partner times you.
Round 1: read from your note cards.
Round 2: glance at the cards only twice.
Round 3: hold the cards but do not look at them.
Write down your three times and circle the closest one to two minutes.
activities.answerSummary: Three recorded times, with round 3 falling between one minute forty seconds and two minutes twenty seconds, and the child able to speak without reading the cards.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Showcase Setup and Celebration
activities.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Measure your part of the display table and mark it with tape.
Set out your booklet, your best page and one picture.
Check the weather chart and agree where the class will move if rain arrives.
Walk the room once as a visitor and read three booklets that are not yours.
activities.answerSummary: Each table space measured and marked, three items set out, a wet weather plan agreed with the class, and three peer booklets read and commented on.

assignments.title: Grade 3 capstone: My Year of Words booklet and showcase presentation
assignments.instructions:
Submit four things: the finished six-page booklet, the marked revision draft, the showcase presentation recording, and your four-sentence reflection with its Grade 4 goal.
Marking guidance for the teacher. Award up to 16 marks for the booklet with the Grade 3 writing rubric, judging ideas, organisation, language and conventions. Award up to 16 marks for the recording with the Grade 3 speaking rubric, judging clarity, vocabulary, sentence accuracy and interaction. Award up to 16 marks for the revision draft: 4 for acting on peer feedback, 4 for accurate past and present verb forms, 4 for varied joining words, and 4 for punctuation including speech marks. Award up to 16 marks for the reflection: 4 for naming something genuinely hard, 4 for naming a real improvement with evidence, 4 for the page chosen and the reason given, and 4 for a Grade 4 goal that names a single action. Credit any booklet whose six pages come from four or more units, and credit any recording that opens, presents three pages, answers two questions and thanks the listener. Return one specific Grade 4 transition target for each learner.
assignments.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

quizzes.question: Which word names the usual weather in a place over a long time?
quizzes.explanation: Climate is the usual weather over many years, while weather is what the sky is like today.
quizzes.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

quizzes.question: Which sentence uses the past simple correctly?
quizzes.options: Last March we built a reading wall. | Last March we builded a reading wall. | Last March we build a reading wall. | Last March we did built a reading wall.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Last March we built a reading wall.
quizzes.explanation: Build is irregular, so its past form is built, and the helper did is not needed here.
quizzes.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

quizzes.question: Which meaning belongs to the word graduate?
quizzes.options: To make something by putting parts together. | To use your ears to hear carefully. | To talk about something with other people. | To finish school or a class.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To finish school or a class.
quizzes.explanation: Amal's class will graduate from Year 3 at the end of term, which means they finish it.
quizzes.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

quizzes.question: Which question is written in the correct order?
quizzes.options: Where you did find that fact in the library? | Where did you find that fact in the library? | Where did you found that fact in the library? | Where find you did that fact in the library?
quizzes.correctAnswer: Where did you find that fact in the library?
quizzes.explanation: The helper did comes before the subject you, and the main verb stays plain: find.
quizzes.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

quizzes.question: The Year 3 Showcase brief has four parts. How many pages must your booklet hold?
quizzes.options: Four pages. | Six pages. | Nine pages. | Twelve pages.
quizzes.explanation: Part 1 of the brief tells you to pick six pages from at least four different units.
quizzes.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

quizzes.question: In "Amal's Year of Words", which page did Amal decide to keep for her showcase?
quizzes.options: Her kindness jar page. | Her family tree page. | Her calendar chart page. | Her big numbers page.
quizzes.explanation: After reading her honest sentence again, Amal decided to keep the kindness jar page.
quizzes.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

quizzes.question: Which sentence compares two months correctly?
quizzes.options: March was more wetter than April. | March was wetter than April. | March was most wet than April. | March was the wettest than April.
quizzes.explanation: Short adjectives add -er to compare two things, and the word more is not used as well.
quizzes.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

quizzes.question: Which word names a person who works on a boat or ship at sea?
quizzes.explanation: A sailor works at sea, while an officer keeps people safe on land and a library holds books.
quizzes.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

quizzes.question: Choose the best joining word: We needed the right size, ______ we measured the long table.
quizzes.explanation: Measuring the table is the result of needing the right size, and so points to a result.
quizzes.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

quizzes.question: On Showcase Day, why does Amal say the climate poster was her hardest page?
quizzes.options: Because she recorded the temperature for seven days. | Because she could not find the right library book anywhere. | Because Leo painted the labels too slowly. | Because the rain moved the showcase into the hall.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Because she recorded the temperature for seven days.
quizzes.explanation: Amal answers Doctor Sarah by naming the seven days of temperature records as the hard part.
quizzes.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

liveSessions.title: Capstone launch and portfolio curation
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring every piece of Year 3 English work you still have, sorted into a single folder.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min: what a capstone is and why we look back; 10 min: teacher models choosing two pages and giving reasons; 15 min: children sort their own work into keep, maybe and not this time; 10 min: pairs explain three choices to each other; 5 min: set the six-page target.
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Activities 1 and label the back of every page you kept with its unit number.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

liveSessions.title: Review words and reading conference
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete the thirty-word review map from Activities 2 and circle the five hardest spellings.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min: rapid word wall from Units 1 to 8; 10 min: teacher models reading the Nine Doors poem aloud; 15 min: paired reading of the poem and the showcase brief; 10 min: one-to-one fluency conference with three children; 5 min: agree each child's reading page.
liveSessions.afterSession: Read your strongest page aloud twice at home and record the second reading.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

liveSessions.title: Planning and drafting the year report
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring your six chosen pages and your Writing 2 plan in page order.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min: reread the four parts of the brief; 10 min: teacher models paragraph one on the board; 15 min: silent drafting of paragraphs one and two; 10 min: children read a paragraph to a partner; 5 min: set the drafting target for the week.
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish paragraph three of your first draft and bring it to the next session.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

liveSessions.title: Grammar repair shop and peer feedback
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete the six grammar workshops and bring your first draft, ready to be read by somebody else.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min: four broken sentences on the board; 10 min: teacher models repairing two of them and naming the rule; 15 min: Grammar Repair Shop in threes; 10 min: peer feedback studio with green and yellow notes; 5 min: each child names the two notes they will act on.
liveSessions.afterSession: Revise your draft using two pieces of feedback and mark every change in a different colour.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

liveSessions.title: Presentation rehearsal with a stopwatch
liveSessions.beforeSession: Make your six numbered note cards and bring your revised booklet.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min: what a strong opening line sounds like; 10 min: teacher models two minutes with note cards; 15 min: three timed rehearsal rounds in pairs; 10 min: surprise questions from the listener; 5 min: each child names one thing to improve tomorrow.
liveSessions.afterSession: Rehearse once more at home and time yourself against the two-minute target.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

liveSessions.title: Showcase exhibition and Year 3 reflection
liveSessions.beforeSession: Bring the finished booklet, your note cards and your display picture, all packed and ready.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min: setting up and measuring table spaces; 10 min: first group presents to visitors; 15 min: remaining groups present in two rounds; 10 min: walk the room and read three other booklets; 5 min: write the Grade 4 goal on the reflection sheet.
liveSessions.afterSession: Hand in the booklet, the reflection and the presentation recording before the last Friday of term.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

teacherNotes.noteType: Capstone delivery, provenance, answer key and device safety
teacherNotes.note:
Where the revision comes from. Every review item in this capstone is traceable to a unit the class has already finished. The thirty words are Units 1 to 8: family, parent, friend and student from Unit 1; teacher, author, library and graduate from Unit 2; January, February, March and April from Unit 3; doctor, officer, sailor and hospital from Unit 4; discuss, listen, offer and build from Unit 5; kind, friendly, honest and kindness from Unit 6; climate, weather, temperature and sunshine from Unit 7; number and million from Unit 8. The six grammar workshops revise, in order: Grammar 1 the past simple from Units 2, 3, 5 and 6; Grammar 2 the present simple and present continuous from Units 1, 3 and 4; Grammar 3 Wh- and Yes/No questions with short answers from Units 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 and 7; Grammar 4 adjectives, comparatives and noun phrases from Units 4, 6 and 7; Grammar 5 the joining words and, but, so and because from Units 1, 4, 5 and 9; Grammar 6 imperatives, reported speech and proofreading from Units 1, 3, 6, 7 and 9. Unit 8 supplies the measuring and counting in the listening text, and Unit 9 supplies the reflection and idea-sharing work in Writing 6 and Activities 4.
Grammar workshop answer key. Grammar 1: listened, built, offered, saw. Grammar 2: checks, is measuring, chooses, are clapping. Grammar 3: How long did Amal record the temperature? When will the class graduate? Has Nora finished her honest reflection? Who built the folder stand with Leo? Grammar 4: wetter, quietest, more helpful, and accept any noun phrase with two adjectives and a comma, such as the long, steady table. Grammar 5: so, but, because, and. Grammar 6: Amal said she was proud of her booklet. The class said they would listen to every plan. Check the title page. Do not rush the last paragraph.
Moderation. Conference at each of the six milestones rather than only at the end, and keep the choices in the child's hands. Mark the booklet and the recording with the shared rubrics, and record one specific Grade 4 transition target for every learner.
Device safety and inclusion. Keep an adult present whenever a child records audio or works with the AI tutor, and never leave a young learner alone with the device. Offer spoken, drawn or written responses so that a child who finds writing hard can still show a full year of progress. Reflection can surface real disappointment about a hard year, so agree in advance who the child may talk to, and always name a genuine strength before a target.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Check the six pages against the unit numbers written on their backs: at least four different units must appear. Reading, writing, speaking and vocabulary evidence must all be present across the set. If a child has five units and no speaking evidence, ask them to swap one page rather than add a seventh.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Unit 1: family, parent, friend, student. Unit 2: teacher, author, library, graduate. Unit 3: January, February, March, April. Unit 4: doctor, officer, sailor, hospital. Unit 5: discuss, listen, offer, build. Unit 6: kind, friendly, honest, kindness. Unit 7: climate, weather, temperature, sunshine. Unit 8: number, million. Accept any four true sentences, and use the five circled words as the child's personal spelling list.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Last March we built the folder stand. She is presenting her booklet right now. Where did you find that fact? The garden is warmer than the hall. The four rules are, in order, irregular past forms, the present continuous helper, question word order, and comparing with -er only.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Each booklet should collect at least two green notes and two yellow notes. A green note must name something specific, not just say good work. A yellow note must be a real question, not a correction. Accept any two notes the writer chooses, provided they can say why those two would improve the booklet most.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Expect three times to be written down. Round 3 should land between one minute forty seconds and two minutes twenty seconds. If round 3 is much shorter, ask the child to add one sentence to each of two pages rather than speak more slowly.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: The table space should be measured and marked before anything is set out. Three items belong on it: the booklet, the best page and one picture. The wet weather plan must name the library. Accept three signed peer comments as evidence that the child walked the room as a visitor.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: climate Climate is the usual weather over many years, while weather is what the sky is like today.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Last March we built a reading wall. Build is irregular, so its past form is built, and the helper did is not needed here.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: To finish school or a class. Amal's class will graduate from Year 3 at the end of term, which means they finish it.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Where did you find that fact in the library? The helper did comes before the subject you, and the main verb stays plain: find.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Six pages. Part 1 of the brief tells you to pick six pages from at least four different units.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Her kindness jar page. After reading her honest sentence again, Amal decided to keep the kindness jar page.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: March was wetter than April. Short adjectives add -er to compare two things, and the word more is not used as well.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: sailor A sailor works at sea, while an officer keeps people safe on land and a library holds books.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: so Measuring the table is the result of needing the right size, and so points to a result.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Because she recorded the temperature for seven days. Amal answers Doctor Sarah by naming the seven days of temperature records as the hard part.
answerKey.origin: Ehel Grade 3 capstone authored from the rebuilt Units 1-9

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the thirty Grade 3 review words correctly when I speak and write.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can answer questions about the capstone texts using evidence from the passage.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use the past simple, questions, comparatives and joining words accurately.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can plan, draft, revise and publish a six-page booklet for real visitors.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can present for two minutes and answer questions from my listeners.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can reflect honestly on Year 3 and set one clear goal for Grade 4.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
